name: TCP
description: Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered byte-stream transport. Establishes a session via the three-way handshake (SYN → SYN/ACK → ACK), tracks every byte with sequence and acknowledgement numbers, retransmits losses, and adapts send rate via flow and congestion control.
securityNotes: SYN-flood DoS, sequence-number prediction (modern stacks randomise), RST injection on unencrypted streams. Pair with TLS for confidentiality.

name: UDP
description: Connectionless, unreliable datagram transport. No handshake, no ordering, no retransmission — just header + payload sent and forgotten. Tiny overhead (8-byte header) makes it ideal where latency matters more than reliability.
securityNotes: Source IP is trivially spoofable, enabling reflection/amplification attacks (DNS, NTP, memcached). Always validate source at the application layer.

name: DHCP
description: Auto-configures hosts joining a network — assigns an IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers, and lease time. Four-step handshake: Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge (DORA).
securityNotes: Rogue DHCP servers can hand out a malicious gateway/DNS for full MITM. DHCP starvation exhausts the pool. Defend with DHCP snooping on switches.

name: ARP
description: Maps a known IPv4 address to the MAC address of the host on the local segment. The asking host broadcasts 'who has 10.0.0.5?'; the owner replies with its MAC. Cached briefly so it doesn't run for every packet.
securityNotes: ARP has no authentication — any host can claim any IP (ARP spoofing / poisoning) to MITM traffic on the LAN. Detect with arpwatch; mitigate with Dynamic ARP Inspection on managed switches.

name: ICMP
description: Control and diagnostic messages for the IP layer — Echo Request/Reply (ping), Time Exceeded (powers traceroute), Destination Unreachable, Redirect, Fragmentation Needed. Not a transport for application data.
securityNotes: ICMP tunnelling smuggles data inside Echo payloads. Smurf attacks use directed broadcasts for amplification. Blocking ICMP entirely breaks PMTU discovery — block selectively.

name: HTTP / HTTPS
description: Stateless request/response protocol for the web. Client sends a method + path + headers + optional body; server replies with a status code + headers + body. HTTPS is the same protocol wrapped in TLS for confidentiality, integrity, and server authentication.
securityNotes: Plain HTTP exposes everything in transit — credentials, cookies, content. Always prefer HTTPS with HSTS. Watch for header injection, request smuggling, mixed content, and weak TLS configurations.

name: SMTP / SMTPS
description: Sends mail between mail servers (port 25) and from clients to their outgoing server (port 587 with STARTTLS, or 465 implicit TLS). Text-based command/response protocol — HELO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA.
securityNotes: Original SMTP has no sender authentication, enabling spoofing. Defend with SPF, DKIM, and DMARC at the domain level; require TLS on submission.

name: POP3 / POP3S
description: Retrieves email from a server to the client, traditionally downloading and deleting messages from the server. Simpler and less stateful than IMAP — designed for one device that owns the inbox.
securityNotes: Plain POP3 sends credentials in cleartext — only use POP3S, or POP3 with STARTTLS. Mostly superseded by IMAP for modern multi-device usage.

name: IMAP / IMAPS
description: Server-side mailbox access — messages live on the server, clients view, search, and organise them across folders. Supports partial fetch, flags (read/unread), and concurrent multi-device access.
securityNotes: Plain IMAP exposes credentials and message content — always use IMAPS or STARTTLS. Enforce app-specific passwords / OAuth where the provider supports them.
useCases: Modern desktop and mobile mail clients

name: DNS
transport: UDP (TCP for large responses and zone transfers)
description: Distributed hierarchical database that resolves human-readable names (example.com) to IP addresses, plus many other record types (MX, TXT, CNAME, NS, AAAA, SRV). Recursive resolvers walk the tree from root → TLD → authoritative server.
securityNotes: Plain DNS is unauthenticated and unencrypted — vulnerable to cache poisoning, on-path tampering, and surveillance. DNSSEC adds origin authentication; DoT (TCP/853) and DoH (HTTPS/443) encrypt the resolver path. DNS tunnelling is a common exfiltration channel.

name: TLS
description: Provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication for application data. Handshake negotiates cipher suite, validates the server certificate (and optionally the client), and derives session keys; bulk data is then encrypted and MAC'd. Successor to SSL.
securityNotes: Disable SSLv3 / TLS 1.0 / TLS 1.1 — only TLS 1.2 (with strong ciphers) and TLS 1.3 should be enabled. Validate certificates rigorously, watch certificate expiry, and consider HSTS plus certificate pinning for sensitive clients.

name: SSH
description: Encrypted, authenticated remote access protocol. The session is built in two stages: the SSH transport layer performs key exchange (typically ECDH / Curve25519), authenticates the server via its host key, and derives session keys; user authentication then runs over the encrypted channel using public keys, passwords, or MFA. Once authenticated, multiple logical channels are multiplexed over the single connection — interactive shells, port forwards, X11, SFTP, and exec commands.
securityNotes: Disable password authentication and enforce key-based or certificate-based auth. Pin and verify host keys (TOFU is brittle — distribute via SSHFP DNS records or an SSH CA). Restrict accounts with AllowUsers / Match blocks, disable root login, and rotate keys. Watch for agent-forwarding abuse on shared hosts; never forward an agent into an untrusted box.
useCases: Remote shell access on Linux / BSD / macOS / network gear
Carrying SFTP and SCP file transfer

name: SFTP
description: File transfer and remote filesystem operations layered on top of an authenticated SSH session. Supports listing, reading, writing, renaming, permissions, and resumable transfers — all over a single encrypted connection.
securityNotes: Inherits SSH security — prefer key-based auth, disable password login, restrict users with chroot or rssh. Confused with FTPS but entirely unrelated.

name: FTPS
description: Classic FTP wrapped in TLS — explicit mode upgrades the existing port-21 connection with AUTH TLS, implicit mode uses TLS from the start on port 990. Retains FTP's separate control and data channels, which complicates NAT and firewall traversal.
securityNotes: Plain FTP sends credentials and data in cleartext and is broken on hostile networks. FTPS fixes the encryption gap but inherits FTP's awkward two-channel design. Prefer SFTP for new deployments.
useCases: Compliance-driven environments still standardised on FTP

name: OSPF
description: Open-standard link-state interior gateway protocol. Routers flood Link-State Advertisements describing their directly connected links; every router in an area builds an identical topology database and runs Dijkstra's algorithm to compute the shortest path to every destination.
securityNotes: Authenticate adjacencies (HMAC-SHA preferred, MD5 acceptable). A rogue or misconfigured OSPF neighbour can inject routes that black-hole or redirect traffic for sniffing.
useCases: Datacenter fabrics (often replaced by BGP at scale)

name: EIGRP
description: Cisco-developed (open since 2013) advanced distance-vector IGP using DUAL — the Diffusing Update Algorithm. Maintains a successor (best path) and feasible successor (pre-computed loop-free backup) per destination, enabling sub-second failover.
securityNotes: Authenticate neighbour relationships with MD5 or HMAC-SHA. Adoption outside Cisco shops is rare; attack surface is mostly inside Cisco-heavy networks.
useCases: Mixed-bandwidth WANs (composite metric handles them well)

name: BGP
description: Path-vector protocol that exchanges routes between Autonomous Systems — the routing protocol of the public internet. Each route carries the full AS-path it has traversed, making loop detection trivial. Path selection is policy-driven via attributes (LOCAL_PREF, AS-PATH length, MED, communities).
securityNotes: BGP hijacking and route leaks regularly cause global outages. Validate origin with RPKI/ROA, filter inbound and outbound prefixes, and authenticate peers (MD5 or TCP-AO).

name: RIP
description: Original distance-vector IGP. Each router broadcasts its full routing table every 30 seconds; neighbours add 1 to each metric and pick the lowest. Hop count is the only metric (max 15; 16 = unreachable). Slow convergence and count-to-infinity behaviour limit it to small networks.
securityNotes: RIPv1 has no authentication and is trivially poisonable. RIPv2 supports MD5 — use it, or avoid RIP entirely in favour of OSPF/EIGRP.

name: VPN
transport: Varies — UDP, TCP, or directly over IP
ports: OpenVPN 1194/UDP, IPsec/IKE 500 + 4500/UDP (NAT-T), WireGuard configurable, SSL VPNs 443/TCP
description: Tunnels network traffic through an authenticated, encrypted channel over an untrusted network — typically the public internet. Modes range from full-tunnel remote access (route everything through HQ) to site-to-site (link two private networks) to split-tunnel.
securityNotes: Protocol choice matters: WireGuard, IPsec/IKEv2, and modern OpenVPN are strong; PPTP and L2TP-without-IPsec are broken. Endpoint compromise compromises the tunnel — pair with MFA, posture checks, and least-privilege routing.
